meta.title: Latest query loses to stale search results
meta.summary: A shared results bucket mixes cache hydrate and network commits, so an older slow query can still repaint the list after the user has moved on.
signals: Cards briefly flash back to an older query
Cache hydrate and network response both write into one results store
Debounce reduced traffic but stale UI still appears on slow network

context.symptom: A user types 'react', then quickly deletes to 're'. The UI shows the latest query, then flashes older 'react' cards because an older commit path still wins briefly.
context.userImpact: Search feels unreliable because the interface does not reflect the most recent intent.
context.environment: Vanilla JS search flow with a 250ms debounce, a recent-query cache, and one shared results store reused by every query path.

evidence.title: Network timeline
evidence.body: Request 17 for 'react' resolves after request 18 for 're', and both end up calling the same commit path for the shared results store.

evidence.title: Current cache + response handling
Observed behavior
evidence.body: Adding a 250ms debounce reduced traffic, but stale flashes still appear on throttled 3G when backspacing quickly.

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.prompt: What is the main failure mode?
stages.helperText: Pick the explanation that matches the stale repaint itself, not just request count or cache presence.

options.label: The debounce window is too short, so the UI ends up showing the wrong query.
options.feedback: A longer debounce might reduce overlap, but it does not fix a commit path that still lets stale work paint the screen.

options.label: Both cache hydrate and network responses write into one shared results bucket without an active-query guard, so older work can still repaint the list.
options.feedback: Correct. This is a take-latest bug in the commit path, not just a traffic problem.

options.label: The browser should use Promise.race so the fastest response always wins.
options.feedback: Fastest is not the same as latest. This framing confuses a race primitive with product intent.

options.label: The main issue is simply that the cache sometimes has old data for the query.
options.feedback: Cache staleness can contribute to the flash, but the deeper bug is that multiple sources can still commit blindly into one live bucket.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: Rank the three most useful debug steps first.
stages.helperText: You are trying to prove exactly where stale work is still allowed to repaint the current query.

candidates.label: Reproduce on slow network while logging query text, source type (cache or network), and request ids together.
Inspect whether cache hydrate and network response paths both write into the same shared results store.
Confirm that the committed payload query differs from the currently active query when the flash happens.
Rewrite the search endpoint to return smaller payloads first.
Increase debounce to 600ms before you understand the race.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which changes belong in the first robust fix?
stages.helperText: Choose the options that make every commit query-aware instead of last-writer-wins.

options.label: Route cache hydrate and network responses through one query-aware commit helper that refuses stale writes.
options.feedback: Correct. The fix needs one guarded commit path instead of multiple blind writers.

options.label: Track a request id or query version and ignore responses that are no longer current.
options.feedback: Correct. This protects you even if abort is not honored all the way down.

options.label: Wrap all requests in Promise.race so the fastest response updates the UI.
options.feedback: Fastest response is explicitly the wrong goal here. Latest intent is what matters.

options.label: Abort or supersede older in-flight requests when a newer query starts.
options.feedback: Correct. This reduces overlap, though the real guarantee still comes from guarded commits.

options.label: Keep only the debounce; it already reduces overlap enough.
options.feedback: Debounce can help load, but alone it does not guarantee correct ordering.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the strongest guardrail for this class of bug?
stages.helperText: Think about something that proves stale responses cannot overwrite fresh intent anymore.

options.label: Reduce API latency budgets so requests are less likely to overlap.
options.feedback: Useful, but lower latency does not replace correctness guarantees in the client.

options.label: Cache every result forever so the client no longer needs to issue new searches.
options.feedback: That changes product behavior and still does not solve stale commit logic.

options.label: Do a quick manual smoke test before release and call it done.
options.feedback: Manual checks are helpful, but weak against timing-sensitive regressions.

options.label: Add an integration test that mixes cached hydrate and delayed network responses, then asserts only the active query can repaint the list.
options.feedback: Correct. That covers the real messy flow here: multiple sources trying to write into one visible list.

scoreBands.label: Race condition not yet contained
scoreBands.summary: You treated overlap as a traffic problem instead of a correctness problem.

scoreBands.label: Mostly correct, missing one safeguard
scoreBands.summary: You saw the race, but the debug order or regression guard did not fully lock it down.

scoreBands.label: Take-latest thinking
scoreBands.summary: You proved the stale repaint, guarded the shared commit path, and tested the exact cache plus network race.

idealRunbook: Reproduce with throttled timing and log cache plus network sources together.
Prove that one shared commit path accepts stale work after newer intent.
Add query-aware guards plus request supersession.
Protect the flow with a mixed cache and delayed-response integration test.

teachingBlocks.title: The easy trap
teachingBlocks.text: Once cache hydrate and network response share the same commit sink, this stops being only a request-volume problem. It becomes a state-ownership problem too.

teachingBlocks.title: Stronger interview framing
items: Name the bug as an async race condition.
Point out the shared commit path instead of only blaming request overlap.
Explain why latest intent must win across both cache and network sources.

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: If you kept the cache, what single commit invariant would you enforce before anything can repaint the result list?
